1: "Keep me safe, my God, for in you I take refuge" — the prayer of the incarnate Son to the Father. John 17 is the extended version of this prayer: "Holy Father, protect them by the power of your name... My prayer is not that you take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one" (John 17:11, 15). Christ both prayed this prayer himself (for himself in Gethsemane, for his disciples in John 17) and answers it for those who take refuge in him.
2: "I say to the LORD, 'You are my Lord; apart from you I have no good thing'" — Christ's absolute dependence on the Father. John 5:19: "the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing." John 5:30: "I can do nothing by myself." The incarnate Son expressed the created dependence of humanity in its perfected form — not a forced dependence but a freely chosen one, grounded in the love between Father and Son. This verse describes what Adam failed to live and what Christ perfectly embodied.
3: "I say of the holy people who are in the land: 'They are the noble ones in whom is all my delight'" — Christ's love for his church. John 15:9: "As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you." Ephesians 5:25-27: Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her "to present her to himself as a radiant church, without stain or wrinkle or any other blemish, but holy and blameless." His delight in the saints ("noble ones") is the Father's delight in the Son extended to all who are united to him (John 17:26: "the love you have for me may be in them").
4: "Those who run after other gods will suffer more and more. I will not pour out libations of blood to such gods or take up their names on my lips" — Christ's absolute rejection of every competing loyalty. "You shall have no other gods before me" was the first commandment that every Israelite failed; Christ alone lived it perfectly. His temptation (Matthew 4:1-11) was specifically the temptation to worship another (Satan's offer of the kingdoms of the world) and he refused. His entire life was the enacted form of this verse.
5: "LORD, you are my portion and my cup; you have made my lot secure" — Christ's contentment in the Father alone. The "portion" language recalls the Levitical priesthood (Numbers 18:20 — "the LORD himself is their inheritance" for the Levites who received no land portion). Christ, as the great High Priest, received the Father as his inheritance rather than earthly kingdoms (Matthew 4:8-10 — he refused the kingdoms offered by Satan). For believers united to Christ, the Father himself becomes the inheritance: "If you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's seed, and heirs according to the promise" (Galatians 3:29).
6: "The boundary lines have fallen for me in pleasant places; surely I have a delightful inheritance" — the perfect contentment of Christ in the Father's provision. This contrasts with Adam's refusal of contentment (Gen 3 — he grasped at what God had not yet given) and the devil's temptation in Matt 4 (offering what the Father had not yet assigned). Christ's "delightful inheritance" is ultimately the resurrection: "You are my Son; today I have become your Father. Ask me, and I will make the nations your inheritance" (Psalms 2:7-8).
7: "I will praise the LORD, who counsels me; even at night my heart instructs me" — Christ's communion with the Father through prayer. Jesus regularly prayed through the night (Luke 6:12) and rose before dawn to pray (Mark 1:35). The "instruction of the heart at night" describes the intimacy of the Father-Son relationship in prayer — a communion that sustained Christ's human nature through its suffering and that he invites his disciples to share ("when you pray, go into your room, close the door and pray to your Father" — Matthew 6:6).
8: "I keep my eyes always on the LORD. With him at my right hand, I will not be shaken" — Peter cites this verse in Acts 2:25-28 explicitly as David speaking of Christ. "He was not shaken" — tested at every point, Christ's orientation toward the Father never wavered (Hebrews 12:2: "For the joy set before him he endured the cross, scorning its shame, and sat down at the right hand of the throne of God"). The unshakeable fixity of Christ on the Father is the pattern for the believer: "fixing our eyes on Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith" (Hebrews 12:2).
9: "Therefore my heart is glad and my tongue rejoices; my body also will rest secure" — Peter quotes this verse in Acts 2:26 as prophetic of Christ's resurrection. The "resting secure" of the body is the assurance against bodily corruption in the grave — the body of Christ would not remain in death. Luke 24:41 records the risen Christ's joy: "while they still did not believe it because of joy and amazement..." The gladness of this verse is specifically the gladness of the resurrection — the body secured against the grave.
10: "...because you will not abandon me to the realm of the dead, nor will you let your faithful one see decay" — the key resurrection prophecy in this psalm range. Peter cites it directly in Acts 2:27, 31: "he seeing this before spoke of the resurrection of the Christ, that his soul was not left in Hades, neither did his flesh see corruption." Paul quotes it in Acts 13:35-37: "as David's body decayed, 'but the one whom God raised from the dead did not see decay.'" This is the central Christological verse of Ps 16: the promise that the "faithful one" (ḥāsîd) — the covenantal loyal one — would not see decay is uniquely fulfilled in Jesus.
11: "You make known to me the path of life; you will fill me with joy in your presence, with eternal pleasures at your right hand" — the resurrection and ascension. "The path of life" — Christ is "the resurrection and the life" (John 11:25), "the way and the truth and the life" (John 14:6). "Joy in your presence" — the joy of the Father's welcome at the ascension (John 17:5: "Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began"). "Eternal pleasures at your right hand" — the heavenly session (Psalms 110:1; Hebrews 1:3; Revelation 3:21). This verse is the end of the path that began at the cross.
